name: McDermitt
short_description: Remote high-desert BLM site near the Oregon border known for opal and chalcedony nodules. Free public rockhounding with gentle terrain at 4600+ feet elevation.
description:
McDermitt sits in the far northern reaches of Nevada's Humboldt County, just miles from the Oregon border in a wide-open high-desert landscape. This BLM-managed area offers free public rockhounding for opal, chalcedony, jasper, and agate specimens scattered across gentle slopes and washes. The location is part of the McDermitt volcanic field, where ancient rhyolitic lava flows and pyroclastic deposits created ideal conditions for silica-rich mineral formation.

The terrain around McDermitt is characterized by sagebrush flats, gentle rolling hills, and scattered volcanic outcrops. At elevation near 4600 feet, the area receives moderate precipitation and seasonal temperature swings. Most productive collecting occurs in dry washes and along eroded hillsides where weathering exposes nodules and fragments. The remoteness of this location means you will encounter very few other collectors and have vast stretches of land to explore.

Best collecting strategy involves walking washes after seasonal runoff and scanning open ground where volcanic material has weathered to the surface. Opal specimens range from common white and milky varieties to occasional fire opal with orange and red play. Chalcedony nodules often appear as rounded stones in tan, gray, and cream colors. The gentle slope makes this accessible for most hikers, though distances from parking can be significant.

This is genuinely remote high-desert rockhounding. Cell service is spotty to nonexistent, roads are rough in places, and the nearest services are 30+ miles away. Come prepared with water, fuel, supplies, and a good map. The reward is solitude, wide-open vistas, and solid collecting opportunities for quality specimens.
history: The McDermitt volcanic field formed during the Oligocene epoch (roughly 20-30 million years ago) when extensive silica-rich rhyolitic volcanism blanketed this region. Hot hydrothermal fluids circulating through fractured volcanic rocks deposited chalcedony, opal, and other silica minerals in cavities and veins, creating the nodule-bearing deposits rockhounders find today.
tips: Search dry washes and arroyos first - seasonal water flow concentrates nodules and fragments into natural collecting areas. Look for rounded, translucent to opaque stones in white, tan, gray, and cream colors; fire opal specimens often show orange or red coloration in strong sunlight. Bring a small hand pick or pry bar to test rocks in place before extracting. Scan the ground systematically walking parallel lines across open flats rather than randomly wandering - nodules are scattered but concentrated enough that methodical searching pays off.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4621 feet in high desert with gentle to moderate slope. Sparse sagebrush, volcanic rock outcrops, and open ground. Most terrain is walkable but rocky underfoot. Distances to productive areas can exceed 1-2 miles from parking. No maintained trails. Unpaved BLM roads are passable in dry conditions but become difficult after rain.
written_directions: From McDermitt town center, take Nevada State Route 292 north toward the Oregon border (roughly 5-10 miles). Look for unmarked BLM pullouts and dirt roads branching east and west from the highway. The main collecting areas spread across open sagebrush country accessible via these side roads. High-clearance or 4WD vehicles are recommended, especially after rain. Park at informal pullouts near washes and exposed volcanic slopes. Exact coordinates 41.918646, -117.812717 marks the general productive zone.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM roads, washboard and rough spots common. High-clearance or 4WD recommended. Passable in dry conditions but impassable after rain or heavy snow.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM roads. No developed facilities. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical spots. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or digging equipment. Collect only from the surface or loose material. Remove all trash. Respect any posted closures or restricted areas.
rules:
Personal use only

No motorized excavation

nearest_services: McDermitt town, approximately 10-15 miles south. Limited services including small grocery and fuel. Larger services 50+ miles in Oregon or south toward Gerlach, Nevada.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with scattered volcanic stones and loose material. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Walking distances from parking to productive areas often exceed 1 mile. Gentle slopes are walkable but footing is uneven. Not suitable for limited mobility.

faq.question: What types of opal can you find rockhounding at McDermitt Nevada?
faq.answer: McDermitt produces both common white and milky opal and occasional fire opal specimens with orange and red play-of-color. Most finds are nodules and small pieces scattered on the surface or in dry washes. The opal forms in cavities within ancient rhyolitic volcanic rocks from the Oligocene epoch.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to collect at McDermitt BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at McDermitt. BLM rules allow personal use collection on public land with a maximum of 25 lbs per day. You must follow FLPMA regulations: no commercial use, no motorized excavation, and surface collection only.

faq.question: How far is McDermitt from the nearest town and services?
faq.answer: McDermitt town is approximately 10-15 miles south of the main collecting areas. The town offers limited services including a small grocery store and fuel. Larger amenities are 50+ miles away in Oregon communities or south toward Gerlach, Nevada. Plan ahead and bring supplies.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding chalcedony and jasper at McDermitt?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal, with late summer and early fall offering the most stable weather and accessible road conditions. Avoid winter months when snow and ice make unpaved BLM roads difficult to impassable. Spring can be wet, turning roads to mud.

faq.question: Is McDermitt rockhounding site good for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, McDermitt is beginner-friendly with gentle terrain and abundant surface specimens. Children ages 8 and up can participate. The main challenge is remoteness and distance from parking areas. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and plan for 2-4 hour collecting trips.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need for rockhounding at McDermitt Nevada?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick or pry bar to test and extract nodules, a collecting bag, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, at least 2-3 liters of water per person, a detailed map, and a compass or GPS unit. Cell service is spotty, so do not rely on your phone for navigation. High-clearance or 4WD vehicles are essential given rough road conditions.

meta_title: McDermitt Rockhounding - Find Opal in Nevada
meta_description: Free public rockhounding at McDermitt BLM land. Collect opal, chalcedony, and jasper on gentle high-desert slopes. No permit required. Remote northern Nevada site.
